name: Sol
description: Sol, also known as Sól or Sunna, is the Norse goddess of the Sun. She is the daughter of Mundilfari and sister of Máni, the god of the Moon. Her primary duty is to drive the chariot of the sun across the sky each day, bringing light and warmth to the world. The chariot is pulled by the horses Árvakr (Early Awake) and Alsviðr (Very Quick). To protect Sol and the world from the intense heat, the gods placed a shield called Svalinn (Cooler) in front of her. Sol is constantly pursued by the wolf Sköll, who seeks to devour her. According to prophecy, Sköll will eventually succeed in swallowing the sun, marking the beginning of Ragnarök, the twilight of the gods. Despite the grim fate that awaits her, Sol bravely fulfills her duty each day, ensuring the cycle of day and night continues. She is revered for her life-giving warmth and the vital role she plays in the natural world. After Ragnarök, a new sun, perhaps her daughter, will rise to continue the cycle. Some accounts suggest she has daughters who will carry on her legacy. Sol represents light, warmth, life, and the cyclical nature of time. Her existence is a constant reminder of the impending doom of Ragnarök, yet also a symbol of hope for renewal and rebirth.
subtitle: The Norse Goddess of the Sun

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Sun and Moon
keyMyths.description: Mundilfari named his children Sun and Moon, angering the gods. As punishment, they were tasked with driving the sun and moon across the sky.

keyMyths.title: The Pursuit of Sköll
keyMyths.description: Sol is constantly chased by the wolf Sköll, who seeks to devour her. This chase explains the cyclical nature of day and night.

keyMyths.title: Sol at Ragnarök
keyMyths.description: During Ragnarök, Sköll will finally catch and devour Sol, plunging the world into darkness. This event marks the beginning of the end.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A primary source for Norse mythology, including descriptions of Sol and her role.
sources.text: A primary source for Norse mythology, including descriptions of Sol and her role.

sources.author: Various
sources.description: A collection of Old Norse poems that contain mythological stories, including references to Sol.
sources.text: A collection of Old Norse poems that contain mythological stories, including references to Sol.

worshipPractices.name: Sun Prayers
worshipPractices.description: Ancient Norse people likely offered prayers to Sol, especially during sunrise and midday, to ensure her continued journey across the sky and the provision of light and warmth. These prayers may have involved offerings of food or other valuable items.

worshipPractices.name: Blóts
worshipPractices.description: While direct evidence of Sol-specific blots (sacrifices) is scarce, it is plausible that she received offerings during general seasonal blots, especially during the summer solstice, to honor the sun's power and ensure a bountiful harvest. Animals or agricultural products may have been sacrificed.

festivals.name: Summer Solstice (Midsummer)
festivals.description: The summer solstice, known as Midsummer, was likely a significant time for honoring Sol. Bonfires were lit to symbolize the sun's power and celebrate the longest day of the year. Feasting, dancing, and other festivities were common.

cultural.modernLegacy: Sol's legacy continues in modern Neopagan and Heathen practices, where she is honored as a source of life, warmth, and energy. Her name is also reflected in the names of days of the week, such as Sunday.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: In Norse cosmology, Sol's origins are rooted in the creation of the world. She is the daughter of Mundilfari, a mortal man who named his children Sunna (Sol) and Máni (Moon) because of their beauty. This act angered the gods, who placed Sol and Máni in the sky to drive the sun and moon chariots, respectively. Sol's primary task is to guide the sun across the heavens each day, bringing light and warmth to the world. This divine appointment was not merely a punishment but a crucial role in maintaining the cosmic order. The Prose Edda and Poetic Edda provide the main sources for her origin story, highlighting the importance of celestial balance in Norse mythology. Her birth and placement in the sky are essential to the Norse understanding of time and the seasons.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The most prominent myth involving Sol is her daily journey across the sky, driving a chariot pulled by the horses Árvakr (Early Awake) and Alsviðr (Very Quick). The god Skoll constantly pursues her, seeking to devour her. This chase explains the phenomenon of solar eclipses; when Skoll gets too close, he partially consumes the sun, causing a temporary darkening of the sky. Sol is destined to be swallowed by Skoll at Ragnarök, the end of the world. However, a new sun, her daughter, will be born to continue the cycle of light and warmth. The protective shield Svalinn is said to protect the earth from being scorched by the sun's heat. These myths highlight the precariousness of the cosmic order and the constant threat of chaos in Norse cosmology. Sol's role is not just about providing light but also about participating in the ongoing battle between order and destruction.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Direct evidence of widespread, organized worship of Sol is limited, but her importance in Norse cosmology suggests that she was likely honored through various rituals and practices. The summer solstice, a time when the sun's power is at its peak, would have been a significant occasion for honoring Sol. Bonfires were likely lit to symbolize the sun's light and warmth, and feasts and celebrations may have been held to ensure a bountiful harvest. While no specific temples dedicated solely to Sol have been discovered, it is possible that she received offerings at general Norse religious sites. Votive offerings, such as sun wheels or solar symbols, may have been dedicated to her. The prevalence of solar symbolism in Norse art and artifacts further suggests the importance of Sol in their religious beliefs. The lack of extensive documentation does not diminish her role as a vital force in the Norse worldview.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Sol's legacy extends beyond ancient Norse mythology and continues to influence modern culture. Her name is preserved in various place names and personal names throughout Scandinavia. The English word "sun" itself is derived from the Proto-Germanic *sōwilō or *sōwuliz, related to Sol's name. In modern Neopagan and Heathen traditions, Sol is honored as a goddess of light, warmth, and life. Her energy is invoked in rituals and practices aimed at promoting growth, healing, and positive change. Solar symbols, such as the sun wheel, are used to represent her power and presence. Sol's story serves as a reminder of the importance of the sun in sustaining life and maintaining balance in the world. Her enduring appeal reflects the universal human fascination with the sun and its life-giving properties. She continues to inspire artists, writers, and spiritual seekers who are drawn to her radiant energy.

shortDescription: Sol, the Norse goddess of the Sun, drives her chariot across the sky daily, bringing light and warmth. She is the sister of Máni, the Moon.
